content:
While searching for supplements online, I stumbled upon a critical UI issue. This minor front-end oversight creates a domino effect that quietly drains the store’s revenue.

2 min read
Just now

--

I landed on the product page directly from Google Search. The layout was clean, the pricing was fair, and the product info was helpful. I was ready to buy and tapped “Add to Cart.” The page reloaded, but nothing seemed to happen. I tapped again — still no visual feedback.
The floating shopping cart icon has completely disappeared from the viewport, leaving the user with no way to access their cart directly from this pageI was ready to make a purchase, but the interface wouldn’t let me. Ultimately, this friction forces me — and up to 85% of mobile shoppers (Data Insight research of the e-commerce in Russia) — to abandon the site.

The Investigation
I decided to dig deeper to find where the cart went. When I navigated back to the homepage, I found my cart containing four identical items. Would an average customer go through this hassle just to complete an order? Unlikely.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Despite the page reloading and shifting slightly with each tap, there is no visual feedback, pop-up confirmation, or visible cart icon anywhere on the screen. The user scrolls up and down the entire page, showing product descriptions, prices, and menus, but a shopping cart button or counter remains completely missing from the mobile viewport.
It turns out the cart icon didn’t actually disappear from the product page. It was simply pushed off-screen. If you manually zoom out the mobile viewport, you can see it sitting outside the layout.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Fix
The root cause was the absence of flex-wrap on the button container. This broke the grid width and pushed the cart element out of the viewport.
As a quick fix, you can switch the flex-direction to column on mobile viewports and ensure overflow-x: hidden is configured correctly (though a proper layout refactoring is always preferred).
